[IMAGE:0]It's just before midnight as I write this, so we can proceed with a new Magic Monday. Let's start things out with a quote again: 
 
"There are many metaphysicians who can look a fact in the face and they deny it with a series of affirmations. [...] There are many pseudo-metaphysicians who could live a thousand years without experiencing anything because their philosophy has taken away the privilege of experience.  When metaphysics teaches you that divine law pervades all things, you must be extremely careful not to resolve this fact into a platitude. Do not start affirming that everything is all right when it evidently is not." 
 
That's from Manly P. Hall's Self-Unfoldment by Disciplines of Realization, arguably Hall's best book and one of the classics of the Golden Age of American occultism. (The image to the left, on the other hand, is one of Augustus Knapp's splendid illustrations for Hall's The Secret Teachings of All Ages.)     

Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. (Any question received after then will not get an answer, and will likely just be deleted.) If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here.
 
I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use the button below to access my online tip jar. If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here.
[IMAGE:1]
With that said, have at it!

***This Magic Monday is now closed. See you next week!***

And don't forget to look up your Pangalactic New Age Soul Signature at CosmicOom.com!

Recently I early-voted for Trump after many years of associating with nihilist Antifa characters. Since doing so, the creeping sense of having become the political enemies of my former comrades has tormented me. As a group at least, they would now consider me to have earned my nine grams of lead to the back of the skull, as the old Soviets liked to joke.

None of these people are now part of my life, but since casting my fateful ballot and joining the ranks of the deplorables I've been depressed to think of what my former friends would likely wish to do with me, if they knew and if they could. Currently, I'm journaling many hours a day working to resolve my spiritual problems. I've practiced a daily banishing ritual, daily meditation, and more than daily divination for several years now and have an active religious life. The operative magic I work is natural magic, strongly influenced by Hoodoo transposed to the polytheistic religious tradition in which I pray. I've got a lot of herbs on hand, know how to make petition papers, anoint candles, and pray over all of the above!

Is it possible that the egregore that I once participated might now torment me since I've "changed sides"? The past few days since voting have proved emotionally taxing and I've had a series of vaguely menacing dreams. Do you know of a magical method --- appropriate to the magic I practice --- to get an egregore you no longer want anything to do with to leave you alone?

comments.content:
1) I recently watched Vivarium, a truly awful horror film, and somewhat bizarrely enough, it raises a few occult related questions: The bulk of the film takes place in a weird alien suburb, Yonder. The sky is an off shade, the sun is slightly aritifical looking, and the clouds look fake, being too perfect; all the houses, stretching for miles, look exactly the same, painted a weird sickly green, with perfectly maintained fake-grass lawns; and there is no evidence anyone lives there; nor are any other living beings there, with the environment appearing to be wholly sterile and artificial.

I was somewhat unsettled to realize I recognized the area: I’ve had dreams of being there more or less my entire life. The film is a new one, so it can’t be that I’ve tapped into something, but it seems the film and I have both tapped into something, which suggests to me that Yonder is some sort of astral location.

a) How do I avoid having dreams where I end up being stuck there? I’m able to avoid entering it if I start a dream elsewhere, but I still sometimes end up starting a dream there; these are unpleasant in the extreme due to the remarkably unpleasant nature of the area. I’ve just started LRM, so I have basically no magical skills as of yet.

b) If this is an astral location other people have been to and are influenced by, does that imply suburbia an attempt to manifest it as much as possible on the physical plane?

c) I stumbled on the film by accident the same day I began efforts at self-improvement, and I then immediately felt a powerful need to watch the film. This was bizarre since I’m usually not much of a movie person; nor am I interested in horror; and nothing about the film seemed like something I’d normally have any interest in. In fact, in a lot of ways Vivarium seemed like the kind of film I would go out of my way to avoid watching. I watched it, found it horrific and deeply unpleasant as I had expected, especially because it opened up old wounds: I grew up in suburbia, and had not let myself process just how miserable an experience that was.

I feel essentially as if by watching the movie I’ve yanked a bandage off a festering wound: it hurt, but now I can get to work cleaning it up, rather than spending weeks or months trying to get to the root of the numerous issues which unfold from this. Is it possible a spirit/god/my higher self/something else guided me to the film for that reason? How common is that sort of thing?

3) I also just started LRM, and have a couple questions here:

a) The daily recollection exercise is proving incredibly difficult. So far, each time I’ve done it, I’ve gotten a terrible stress response from it, enough it disrupts my sleep. I’ve taken to doing it a couple hours before I plan to go to bed, then doing some relaxation, and it seems to work. However, I’m a surprised to find it so stressful. Is this normal?

b) Are there things I can do to make it easier? I’m assuming that part of it is just keep at it until I get used to it, but are there other steps I can take?

c) I was quite surprised when I read the first chapter of LRM that there was not a single ritual in there. When I thought about it, I realized that this makes sense: ritual work requires intense focus, and a lot of people (including me!) have trouble with that; the first lesson doesn’t include any rituals since most people lack key skills to be able to do it when they start, and the lesson focuses on building those before introducing any ritual. Am I on the right track with this?

d) I’ve built the tree off life a couple times, and am curious about one point: there is a gap between Tiphareth and Kether, where it feels like an additional sphere. Is this something which will be explored later in the course?

4) I’ve started looking into the weird Kek thing, partially inspired by Feels Good Man, and something I find fascinating is just how much effort people are putting into arguing that no one actually takes Kek seriously; that it’s all an elaborate joke on the part of 4chan, or to avoid talking about it at all. Do you have any idea what’s going on here? It seems absolutely bizarre to me how much effort is being spent to pretend nothing is happening….

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) That's really strange. Still, these things happen; you may need to face the ugly memories of your time in suburbia so you can let go of the emotional burden. (I certainly did.) Don't try to suppress the dreams; journaling about them may be helpful.

3) Many people find it stressful, yes. Doing it well before bed is a good way around that; other than that, just keep at it. As for your insight into the first lesson, square on target -- and now you have a better idea of what you need to do. As for the gap between Kether and Tiphareth, that'll be filled in later on, though not in that very basic course.

4) The two ultimate dogmas of the age of reason are that gods don't exist and magic doesn't work. The Kek Wars demonstrate that both of these dogmas are false. No wonder people are freaking out!

I was wondering about the stress reaction, but it suddenly makes sense: in order to survive my childhood in suburbia, I trained myself to ignore the realities of my surroundings. Unlearning that is going to take quite a lot of work; and right now, anything which forces me to look closely at my surroundings will trigger a panic response, since I'm directly challenging a coping mechanism I needed for more than a decade!

As for intense focus, I'm sure it will come with practice. For now though, I just need to work at the basics, and slowly make my way towards mastery of ritual magic.

comments.content:
"When metaphysics teaches you that divine law pervades all things, you must be extremely careful not to resolve this fact into a platitude. Do not start affirming that everything is all right when it evidently is not."

This seems to tie into a lot of the discussions here about karma. The argument that believing in karma means believing people deserve what they get, while true on one level, obscures another important one: you have the choice on what to do when you see people suffering. You can try to help, or you can do nothing. Either way, sure, karma partially determines what happens, but it's not the be all and end all of it.

Another important part of this, to my mind, is that just because the traditional teachings say that the universe is the way it is for a reason, doesn't mean that we have to put up with things as they are: sometimes, the lesson we need to learn is that some things need to change!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Exactly. Everything is there for a reason, but the reason may be to encourage you to get off your rump and change things!

comments.title: The Johnny Appleseed Report
comments.content:
Hi John
Well… I was hoping for a second report of nothing to report… but… Friday, I started to read “In Praise of Johnny Appleseed” as usual after opening the grove, asking blessings, &c. As I was reading, the Not for Hire faded in – very clearly this time as an ordinary side-wheeler on the Ohio. Yeaa, no drama this time… I thought.
Then – Teddy Roosevelt rode by on a giant sturgeon. Interpretations welcome.
Interestingly, as soon as I thought of the Rough Riders, I started gasping for air. So, I stopped, thought of TR’s diplomatic skill in avoiding a range war and brokering the end of the Russo-Japanese war. (Yankees like me mend fences, right?) Able to breathe again, I finished the poem without further incident. Hard to pray for peace while screaming for war, no?
All this makes me wonder what the curse-hurlers are doing to themselves. And HOW? I can't get a few lines out with the wrong attitude.
Might you comment on progress of the rite at your end?
And – Anybody else having as wild an experience as I am?
Rusty
In other news – the last Presidential debate was watched widely among my blue-collar co-workers. The NASCAR fans again applied their love of complicated rules and metrics to their commentary – which ended up being as good as policy level discussions I’ve heard at university cocktail parties.
And, following a suggestion to add cayenne to the salt and nail in a protective red bag – I didn’t have any, so crushed red pepper went into a sewn bag instead of my usual tied one. Will report next month. Not sure if starting today was a good idea… on the other hand, the work was routine… and the components sort of assembled themselves… as usual. Over to you.

I found out about it by reading old periodicals issued by the Independent Order of Odd Fellows, and reading old books on fraternal lodges generally. It's been quite a while -- my active involvement in the Odd Fellows was in 1992-2004 -- and I don't have access to those sources.

The basic gimmick, though, was that there were lots of lodges around in the late 19th and early 20th centuries, and one common way for them to spread was for representatives of the grand lodge to travel to areas that didn't have one yet and see if they could find a group of people interested in organizing one. That was considered quite legitimate, so long as the GL representative didn't misrepresent the organization. Of course there were plenty of con artists who got some brochures, arranged for a friend in some big city to field mail for the bogus Grand Lodge, and went around recruiting members and collecting initiation fees for orders that didn't exist. Sometimes the bogus order made vast claims about its age, importance, wealth, etc. -- like the Priory of Sion. (Or the Ordo Templi Orientis under Crowley -- I've seen a pamphlet he put out with all sorts of bogus claims, including photos of a big manor house the OTO supposedly owned. As usual with Crowley, it was so over the top that he got few takers.)

comments.title: Affirmations
comments.content:
I formulated two affirmations. I will modify the exact phrasing I used here to "keep silent" but I'm just wondering if this is alright:

1. I'm awesome at knowing what's crucial to do and doing it with concentration.

2. I have a six pack, bench press 1.5x my bodyweight, and have great flexibility and endurance.

Thanks!

replies.title: Re: Affirmations
replies.content:
The first one isn't the way I'd phrase it -- I'd leave out "awesome" and just say something like "I know what must be done and do it with perfect concentration." But the basic idea is good.

The second, though, is a bit of a mess. To say "I have a six pack" could be satisfied by beer in the fridge -- always make your affirmations so they point to one thing only! Bench pressing 1.5x your body weight is a fine goal, but the other two are vague. What I'd suggest here is to combine affirmation and imagination. Use something very simple: "I am strong," or better still, "I am mighty!" (Exaggeration is not a problem in this kind of work.) Then, as you repeat it, imagine your body as you want it to be -- ripped abs and the rest of it -- and see yourself picking up a barbell loaded to the breaking point and doing a perfect clean and jerk with it, with ease. (That specific lift tends to be hardwired into most people's imaginations as the proof of outrageous strength.) Work with that for a while, following it up with plenty of hard workouts, and I think you'll be satisfied with the results.

comments.title: midieval glass
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I am working my way through the poem Preiddeu Annwn for my daily discursive meditations and I have a question for you about some of the symbolism. My understanding is that the poem was written in the 9th century. There are repeated references to glass--a glass castle and Arthur's glass ship Prydwen.

I looked into this and apparently glass was quite rare in western europe until centuries later. So, beyond the obvious implications of castles and ships made from translucent glass, are there special properties of glass that a medieval audience would have assumed? I wondered if it was an alchemical reference, for instance.

replies.title: Re: midieval glass
replies.content: There was plenty of glass in Roman Britain -- the Roman world used glass extensively for drinking vessels, containers, and even windows (glaziers in Alexandria figured out how to make clear glass by 100 BC, and exported fantastic amounts of it). Glass production dropped off sharply in the Dark Ages that followed, but once the Anglo-Saxon kingdoms became more or less stable glass became fairly common again. So glass was by no means rare when the Preiddeu Annwn was written. My guess is that it drew on older traditions, from the days when glass vessels were treasures handed down from Roman times, and the idea of a glass castle meant roughly what a castle of solid gold would represent today.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hello JMG,

In astrology, what's the difference between the following types of energy:

1) Benefic planet applying an easy aspect to a planet
2) Benefic planet applying a hard aspect to a planet
3) Malefic planet applying an easy aspect to a planet
4) Malefic planet applying a hard aspect to a planet

Where benefic planets are Venus and Jupiter, malefic planets are Mars and Saturn, easy aspects are trine or sextile, and hard aspects are square or opposition. The conjunction can be either easy or hard depending on the nature of the planets involved.

As I understand it, in (1), the energy of the benefic planet flows harmoniously and influences the energy of the affected planet. Similarly, in (3), the energy of the malefic planet flows harmoniously and influences the energy of the affected planet (in this case, the harmonious flow is not a good thing, because it makes it easier for the malefic energy to be transmitted). The flow is harmonious because it's an easy aspect. The nature of the energy is determined by the nature of the planet. A Jupiterian energy flowing harmoniously will be different from a Saturnian energy.

However, the situation is less clear for me when we are dealing with the hard aspects. In (2), the energy of the benefic planet conflicts or opposes the affected planet, while in (4), the energy of the malefic planet conflicts or opposes the affected planet. But then how is (4) different from (3)? Does the hard aspect with the malefic planet somehow negate the malefic energy, making it more difficult for it to be transmitted?

Any clarification would be welcome.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Trying to understand this in terms of abstract categories is a great way to get confused. Instead, start with concrete examples. Your own natal chart is the traditional starting place. Look at the aspects in your chart, favorable and unfavorable, from benefics and malefics, and then explore how those work out in your own life and personality. Go on to other charts of people you know well, or famous people whose lives are well documented -- you can learn an enormous amount by reading a good biography of someone with their birth chart close at hand. To say "this kind of aspect always means that" results in inaccurate delineations; to look at the chart as a whole and see how this Mars trine Jupiter echoes the other Mars aspects you've studied is to get better results.

replies.title: Graves
replies.content:
Re The White Goddess-- this is a work of poetic imagination. Graves did not know any Celtic languages (although his grandfather had been a noted scholar) The White Goddess is a fascinating work, but it reflects more about Graves peculiar fascination with the idea of the Goddess as a muse, of her embrace of and eventual rejection of the poet (male you will note) and various other fantasies that influenced both his poetry and his personal life. I actually met the woman who had been his final muse (i.e. she had, when he was in his 80s and she was 16--I think--been his mistress). She was an attendee at the 1998 conference celebrating the 50th anniversary of the work. Her contribution was a sort of interpretive dance of the white goddess of inspiration transitioning to the black goddess of death. If you are interested in reading the White Goddess the Carcanet edition published in England as part of Graves collected works contains his final changes, which I believe the US editions do not.

Rita

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
You need a pinch of salt, a pinch of incense ash, and a glass of water. The salt and ash should each be on their own little plate, or a small piece of paper. Hold your right hand over the salt, palm down, and visualize a stream of white light descending from your palm into the salt. Say:

"May wisdom abide in this salt and may it preserve my mind and body from all corruption. May all phantoms depart from it so that it may become a heavenly salt, salt of earth and earth of salt. May it feed the threshing ox and strengthen my hope with the horns of the Winged Bull. So be it!"

Do the same with the ash. Say:

"May this ash return to the fount of living waters; may it become a fertile earth; may it bring forth the Tree of Life."

Tip salt and ash together into the water, saying:

"In the salt of eternal wisdom, in the water of preparation, in the ash whence the new earth springeth, be all things accomplished unto the Age of Ages. So be it!"

That completes the ritual. You can say "Amen" in place of "So be it" -- that's a recognized variant.

replies.title: Re: Divination
replies.content: The difficulty is that how to ask questions is a very personal matter. comments.title: Lifting weights and occult
comments.content: Hi JMG, I went to the gym today and it got me thinking that I'd never had any interest in the occult until a couple of years after I started lifting weights. replies.title: Re: Lifting weights and occult
replies.content: There used to be. In the early 20th century, the golden age of the physical culture movement, you had a lot of weightlifters, strongmen, and exercise teachers who were also occultists. Joseph Greenstein, the Mighty Atom, was a Jewish Cabalist who used esoteric means to develop the strength he used to tie iron horseshoes into overhand knots with his bare hands; Genevieve Stebbins, who was a very influential teacher of women's exercise, was also a high-ranking adept in the Hermetic Brotherhood of Luxor -- and those are just the two who come first to mind. There were many others. Their secrets have apparently been lost, or at least nobody I know of has been able to dig them up yet, but they can be recovered by the simple expedient of doing the same thing they did -- studying occultism and exercise together.
